architecture.summary: Tree-of-Thought (ToT) augments an auto-regressive LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo) with four external modules that enable multi-round conversational problem-solving with backtracking: a prompter agent generates structured prompts from memory; a checker module (rule-based or neural) validates intermediate solutions; a memory module records conversation history and search tree state; and a ToT controller (rule-based or learned via policy gradient) decides when to backtrack or continue exploration. The LLM generates short-range reasoning steps (next intermediate solution) guided by prompts; the external system orchestrates tree search, error recovery, and state management.
trained_components: ToT controller policy network (optional, policy-gradient trained)
Prompter agent policy network (optional, policy-gradient trained)

claims.statement: For 3x3 Sudoku puzzles, the ToT-based solver was able to solve all puzzles (success rate = 1.0), improving by 11% compared to the second best (few-shot CoT solver)
For 4x4 Sudoku puzzles, the ToT-based solver achieved 90% success rate (9 out of 10 solved), which is 80% higher than one-shot and few-shot solvers at approximately 50%
For 5x5 Sudoku puzzles, the ToT-based solver achieved 80% success rate (8 out of 10 solved), which is 60% higher than one-shot and few-shot solvers at approximately 50%
The ToT framework augments an LLM with a prompter agent, checker module, memory module, and ToT controller to enable tree-like exploration with backtracking for multi-round problem-solving
The checker module can be implemented either as rule-based (for NP problems with explicit polynomial-time verification algorithms like Sudoku) or as a trained neural network classifier
The ToT controller can be implemented as simple rule-based heuristics (backtrack on invalid node or after exploring >5 children) or as a learned policy network trained with REINFORCE

The rule-based ToT controller lacks awareness of whether a partially-filled board can be completed without violating Sudoku rules, decreasing solution search efficiency and causing failures on 4x4 (1 failure) and 5x5 (2 failures) puzzles before reaching maximum 100 conversation rounds

The rule-based checker requires custom domain-specific logic (e.g. Sudoku rule validation), limiting adaptability to other problem types; generalization to mathematical reasoning or logical problems where rule-based checking is difficult remains as future work
